saida_bruta:
The man stirring awake on the sleeping-porch of a Dutch Colonial house, in the Zenith residential section called Floral Heights, had nothing of the giant about him.

His name was George F. Babbitt. He was now forty-six, in April 1920, and he produced nothing in particular—no butter, no shoes, no poetry—but was skillful at the trade of selling houses for more than people could afford.

His broad head was pink, his brown hair thin and dry. His face looked childlike in sleep, despite its wrinkles and the red dents left by his spectacles on his nose. He wasn't fat, but he was very well fed; his cheeks were soft pads, and the smooth hand resting limply on the khaki blanket was faintly puffy. He looked prosperous, thoroughly married, and unromantic; and just as unromantic was this sleeping-porch, overlooking one decent-sized elm, two tidy grass-plots, a cement driveway, and a corrugated iron garage. Yet Babbitt was dreaming again of the fairy child, a dream far more romantic than crimson pagodas beside a silver sea.
